completed.description: Use a for loop to iterate over a list and act on each item

context.text: Say we have names = ['Ana', 'Bo', 'Cy']. How would you greet each person with a loop?
for name in names: print(f"Hello, {name}!")
That runs perfectly — one greeting per person. Why did you pick 'name' as the loop variable?
It just holds each item from the list one at a time, so a singular word made sense.
Exactly right, the loop variable takes each value in turn.
Yeah, and the f-string fills in whichever name it's holding that round.

remaining.description: Use f-strings to insert variables into output text
Use a while loop that stops on a condition
Append items to a list with append()

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student wrote print(f\"Hello, {name}!\") themselves and explained that the f-string fills in whichever name the variable holds."}]}

completed.description: Filter rows with a WHERE clause

context.text: The products table has name and price columns. Pull the ones costing more than 20.
SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE price > 20
Good — that keeps only the expensive rows. Anything you'd add to make the list easier to scan?
Maybe show the priciest first? SELECT name, price FROM products WHERE price > 20 ORDER BY price DESC
That works exactly as you'd hope — descending by price.
Nice, DESC was a lucky guess but it makes sense.

remaining.description: Group rows and aggregate with GROUP BY
Sort query results with ORDER BY
Combine two tables with a JOIN

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m3","evidence":"The student themselves added ORDER BY price DESC to their query, saying 'Maybe show the priciest first?' \u2014 a working sort they wrote unprompted."}]}

completed.description: Add two fractions that have unlike denominators

context.text: Let's try 3/4 + 5/6. What's your first move?
I need a common bottom number. The LCD of 4 and 6 is 12, since 12 is the smallest number both divide into.
Perfect start. So what do the fractions become?
3/4 turns into 9/12 and 5/6 turns into 10/12, so together that's 19/12.
Spot on — 19/12, or 1 and 7/12 as a mixed number.
Got it, so I always convert first and only then add the tops.

remaining.description: Find the least common denominator of two fractions
Multiply two fractions together

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student said 'The LCD of 4 and 6 is 12, since 12 is the smallest number both divide into' \u2014 finding the least common denominator themselves."}]}

completed.description: Conjugate regular -ar verbs in the present tense

context.text: Take hablar. Walk me through the present tense forms.
yo hablo, tu hablas, el habla, nosotros hablamos, ellos hablan
All five correct. Do you see the pattern in the endings?
Yeah, you chop off -ar and add o, as, a, amos, an. Actually I bet comer works the same way: yo como, tu comes, el come, nosotros comemos, ellos comen.
You just conjugated an -er verb perfectly on your own!
Ha, the endings just swap the a for an e mostly.

remaining.description: Conjugate regular -er verbs in the present tense
Choose correctly between ser and estar
Form negative sentences with no

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student volunteered a full correct conjugation of comer: 'yo como, tu comes, el come, nosotros comemos, ellos comen'."}]}

completed.description: Add items to an array with push

context.text: You have let nums = [3, 7]. Add the number 11 to the end.
nums.push(11)
Right — nums is now [3, 7, 11]. What does push return, by the way?
The new length? So 3 here.
Correct. And if you wanted every number doubled into a new array?
Oh I know a shortcut for that: let doubled = nums.map(n => n * 2) — that gives [6, 14, 22] without touching nums.
Exactly, and completely correct that the original stays unchanged.
map is honestly my favorite one.

remaining.description: Transform every element of an array with map
Keep matching elements with filter
Combine array elements into one value with reduce

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student wrote 'let doubled = nums.map(n => n * 2) \u2014 that gives [6, 14, 22] without touching nums', a correct map transformation with the right result."}]}

completed.description: Calculate the mean of a small data set

context.text: Here are quiz scores: 12, 5, 9, 3, 7. What's the mean?
They add to 36, divided by 5 scores is 7.2.
Correct. Does 7.2 have to be one of the actual scores?
No, it's just the balance point. Funny enough the median IS an actual score here — sorted it's 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, so the middle one is 7.
You're right on both counts — mean 7.2, and 7 sits dead center.
Cool, they're close but not the same thing.

remaining.description: Find the median of a data set
Compute the range of a data set
Identify the mode of a data set

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"Unprompted, the student sorted the data '3, 5, 7, 9, 12' and correctly identified 'the middle one is 7' as the median."}]}

completed.description: Total a range of cells with the SUM function

context.text: Column B holds March sales in B2 through B10. Total them in B12.
=SUM(B2:B10)
That's it. What would happen if a cell in that range were blank?
SUM just skips it, treats it like zero. And if I wanted the typical sale instead of the total I'd put =AVERAGE(B2:B10) in the next cell.
Both statements are exactly right.
These range functions all seem to share the same shape.

remaining.description: Compute a mean with the AVERAGE function
Return conditional values with the IF function

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student wrote '=AVERAGE(B2:B10)' themselves, correctly explaining it gives the typical sale instead of the total."}]}

completed.description: Create an unordered list with ul and li tags

context.text: Make a shopping list with milk and bread as items.

• milk
  • bread
Well structured. How would a reader jump from your page to another site?
With a link — like visit my site. The href is where it goes and the text between the tags is what you click.
That's a complete, correct anchor element.
The tag letter 'a' threw me off at first but it stands for anchor.

remaining.description: Create hyperlinks with the anchor tag and href
Embed images with the img tag and src
Build an ordered list with ol

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student wrote 'visit my site' and correctly explained that href is the destination and the inner text is clickable."}]}

completed.description: Balance a simple chemical equation by adjusting coefficients

context.text: Balance H2 + O2 -> H2O.
2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O. To check it: in Ca(OH)2 style counting, the left has 4 H and 2 O, and the right has 4 H and 2 O too.
Balanced perfectly. Why can't we just change H2O to H2O2 instead?
Because that changes the substance itself — hydrogen peroxide isn't water. We're only allowed to change the big numbers in front.
Exactly, coefficients only, never subscripts.
Right, subscripts define what the molecule IS.
Quick side check — how many of each atom are in one unit of Ca(OH)2?
1 calcium, 2 oxygens, and 2 hydrogens — the 2 outside the parentheses multiplies everything inside.

remaining.description: Count atoms of each element in a chemical formula with parentheses
Classify reactions as synthesis, decomposition, or combustion

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student counted Ca(OH)2 correctly: '1 calcium, 2 oxygens, and 2 hydrogens \u2014 the 2 outside the parentheses multiplies everything inside'."}]}

completed.description: Tell whole steps apart from half steps on a keyboard

context.text: From C to D — whole step or half step?
Whole step, there's a black key between them. E to F is the half step since nothing sits between.
Both right. So how far is G to A?
Whole step again. Actually that means the whole C major scale goes C D E F G A B C — whole whole half whole whole whole half.
You just spelled out the entire major scale formula correctly.
It clicks once you see where the two half steps hide.

remaining.description: Build a major scale using the whole and half step pattern
Name intervals like thirds and fifths
Identify sharps and flats in key signatures

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student produced the full scale with its step pattern themselves: 'C D E F G A B C \u2014 whole whole half whole whole whole half'."}]}

completed.description: Rearrange the speed formula v = d/t to solve for time

context.text: A train covers 300 km at 100 km/h. How long does the trip take?
t = d/v, so 300 divided by 100 is 3 hours.
Right — you flipped the formula correctly. What did you do algebraically to get t alone?
Multiplied both sides by t, then divided both sides by v. Going the other way is even easier: if a cyclist rides at 15 km/h for 2 hours, d = v times t = 30 km.
Correct again — 30 km, and the rearrangement was flawless.
These three just rotate around each other.

remaining.description: Calculate distance travelled from speed and time
Convert speeds between km/h and m/s

output: {"alsoAchieved":[{"id":"m2","evidence":"The student computed a distance on their own: 'if a cyclist rides at 15 km/h for 2 hours, d = v times t = 30 km'."}]}

completed.description: Explain what a variable stores and how assignment works

context.text: If I write age = 12, what just happened?
The name age now points at the value 12, like a labeled box.
Nice image. And after age = age + 1?
It becomes 13 — the right side is computed first, then stored back.
Exactly. Later we'll feed variables like this into loops, which repeat work for us.
Cool, loops sound fun.

remaining.description: Write a for loop over a range of numbers
Branch with if and else

completed.description: Read a value out of a dictionary using its key

context.text: Given prices = {'tea': 3, 'coffee': 4}, how do you get the price of tea?
prices['tea'] — that gives 3.
Right. What if the key isn't there?
You get a KeyError. I think I've written functions before at my old job, by the way, so some of this rings a bell.
Good to hear — we'll get to functions soon. For now, how would you look up coffee?
prices['coffee'], which is 4.

remaining.description: Define a function with def and parameters
Return a value from a function
Loop over dictionary keys and values

completed.description: Attach a click event listener to a button

context.text: Here's the setup I'll give you: const btn = document.querySelector('#save'). Now make a click on it log 'saved!'.
btn.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('saved!'))
Works perfectly. What's the first argument there?
The event type, 'click' as a string.
Right. And the querySelector line I gave you — that's how we grabbed the element.
Yeah, document.querySelector('#save') — the # means it finds it by id, like you showed.
Exactly as I showed, yes.
Is this basically the same as onclick in the HTML?

remaining.description: Select DOM elements with querySelector
Prevent default form submission with preventDefault

completed.description: Select specific columns from a table

context.text: The riders table has name, city, and age. Get just names and cities.
SELECT name, city FROM riders
Clean. Why not SELECT * here?
Star drags along columns we don't need, so it's wasteful.
Exactly. Next up we'll meet WHERE, which lets you keep only certain rows — say, riders from one city.
Got it, sounds useful.

remaining.description: Filter rows with a WHERE clause
Match text patterns with LIKE

completed.description: Count rows in a table with COUNT

context.text: How many orders are in the orders table?
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders
That's the one. What does the star mean inside COUNT?
Count whole rows, don't care about any particular column.
Right.
Could I also count only the orders from Berlin? Like with some kind of filter on the city?
Great instinct — that's exactly where we're headed next.
Okay, looking forward to it.

remaining.description: Filter rows with a WHERE clause
Group rows by a column with GROUP BY
Rename output columns with AS

completed.description: Structure a page with header, main, and footer elements

context.text: Sketch the skeleton of a blog page using semantic tags.
<header>site title</header><main>the posts</main><footer>copyright</footer>
Solid skeleton. Why use these instead of three divs?
Screen readers and search engines understand what each region is for.
Exactly.
I styled my blog once with colors and fonts, so hopefully the CSS part will feel familiar.
It probably will! That's coming right up.
Great.

remaining.description: Apply colors and fonts with CSS rules
Target elements with class and id selectors

completed.description: Reference other cells inside a formula

context.text: Cell A1 holds hours, B1 holds hourly rate. Compute pay in C1.
Correct. If A1 is 8 and B1 is 25, C1 shows?
Right. A taste of what's next: =IF(C1>150,"bonus","standard") would label big paydays automatically.
Ooh, that IF thing looks handy.

remaining.description: Return different values based on a condition with IF
Look up values in a table with VLOOKUP

completed.description: Distinguish a population from a sample

context.text: A researcher surveys 200 students out of a university of 30,000. Which is which?
The 30,000 are the population, the 200 are the sample.
Correct. Why sample at all?
Asking everyone costs too much time and money.
Exactly.
And then the mean is like the average of whatever the sample answered, right?
That's the idea — computing it properly is our next stop.
Sounds good.

remaining.description: Calculate the mean of a data set
Calculate the standard deviation of a sample

completed.description: Simplify expressions by combining like terms

context.text: Simplify 3x + 2x + 4.
Good. Why can't the 4 join the 5x?
It has no x — different kind of term, like apples and oranges.
Exactly. Try 7y - 2y + y.
Right again.
So an equation like 5x + 4 = 14 would just be more of the same combining, I guess?

remaining.description: Solve one-step equations for the unknown
Distribute a number across parentheses

completed.description: Choose the definite article el or la for a noun

context.text: Is it el libro or la libro?
el libro — libro is masculine.
Right. And the word for house?
la casa. Hmm, so for 'a house' it would be... un casa?
Close, but not quite — casa is feminine, so it takes una: una casa.
Ah okay, una casa. The gender carries over.
It does. But back to definite articles — what about 'the problem', el problema or la problema?
Tricky one... el problema, it's one of those -ma exceptions.

remaining.description: Choose the indefinite article un or una for a noun
Form plural nouns and articles

completed.description: Tell elements apart from compounds

context.text: Oxygen gas, water, iron — which are elements and which are compounds?
Oxygen and iron are elements, water is a compound because it's hydrogen bonded to oxygen.
Exactly right. Where would you look up all the known elements?
The periodic table. I remember the rows are called periods.
They are! We'll dig into reading it properly next.
Nice, I've always wanted to actually understand that chart.

remaining.description: Find an element's atomic number on the periodic table
Write chemical formulas for simple compounds

completed.description: State that force equals mass times acceleration

context.text: A 2 kg cart accelerates at 3 m/s squared. What force acts on it?
F = ma, so 6 newtons.
Correct. Double the mass, same force — what happens to acceleration?
It halves, 1.5 m/s squared.
Exactly.
So when things fall, heavier things fall faster because gravity pulls them with more force, right?
Careful — that's one of the most famous traps in physics, and it's where we're going next.
Ha, okay, now I'm curious.

remaining.description: Explain why acceleration due to gravity is the same for all masses
Calculate weight from mass and gravity

completed.description: Read note names on the treble clef

context.text: What note sits on the bottom line of the treble clef?
E.
Yes. And the note in the second space up?
A — the spaces spell FACE.
Perfect mnemonic. One more: top line?
F. The bass clef is the other one with the two dots, right? I haven't learned its notes.
That's the one, and it's exactly what we tackle next.
Cool.

remaining.description: Read note names on the bass clef
Identify note durations like quarter and half notes

completed.description: Identify the thesis statement of an essay

context.text: In the school-uniforms essay you read, which sentence is the thesis?
The last sentence of the intro: 'Uniforms should be required because they reduce distraction, cost, and bullying.'
Right — and notice it previews three reasons. What makes it a thesis rather than just a fact?
It takes an arguable position the rest of the essay has to defend.
Exactly.
Topic sentences are kind of like mini-theses for each paragraph, I'm guessing?
Nice intuition — writing those is our very next step.
Looking forward to it.

remaining.description: Write topic sentences for body paragraphs
Use transitions to connect paragraphs

completed.description: Branch program flow with if and else

context.text: Write code that prints 'hot' when temp is above 30 and 'fine' otherwise.
Exactly right. For three or more cases you'd slot elif between them, like: if temp > 30 ... elif temp > 15 ... else.
So elif goes in the middle, between the if and the else.
That's where it lives, yes. But first — what prints when temp is 30 exactly, with your code?
'fine', because 30 is not strictly greater than 30.

remaining.description: Handle multiple branches with elif
Combine conditions with and / or

completed.description: Explain what an array is and how indexes work

context.text: What is ['red', 'green', 'blue'] and how do I get 'green' out of it?
It's an array — an ordered list of values. 'green' is at index 1 because counting starts at zero.
Exactly. What's at index 3?
Nothing — undefined, the array ends at index 2.
Right.
Objects are the ones with the curly braces, I've seen those around in code before.
You have good eyes — objects are exactly where we go next.
Cool cool.

remaining.description: Create an object with named properties
Loop over array elements with for...of

completed.description: Insert new rows with INSERT INTO

context.text: Add a customer named Mira from Lyon to the customers table (columns: name, city).
INSERT INTO customers (name, city) VALUES ('Mira', 'Lyon')
Perfect. Why list the column names explicitly?
So the values land in the right columns even if the table order changes.
Exactly.
And to change her city later I'd probably use, like, an EDIT command or something?
Close in spirit — the real keyword is our next topic.
Ha, okay, I was guessing.

remaining.description: Modify existing rows with UPDATE and SET
Remove rows with DELETE

completed.description: Sort spreadsheet data ascending or descending

context.text: You want the sales sheet ordered from biggest deal to smallest. What do you do?
Select the data, then Data menu, Sort, choose the amount column, descending.
Exactly. And what's the danger of sorting only one column by itself?
The rows get scrambled — amounts stop matching their customer names.
Right, always sort the whole range.
Filtering is under that same Data tab too, I think — I've seen the little funnel icon.
It is, and that funnel is exactly what we look at next.
Neat.

remaining.description: Filter rows with AutoFilter to show matching data
Freeze header rows while scrolling

completed.description: Calculate the probability of a single event

context.text: What's the probability of rolling a 6 on a fair die?
1/6 — one winning face out of six equally likely ones.
Correct. And the probability of rolling an even number?
Nice. Sneak preview: what about rolling two 6s in a row?
No idea, that's the next thing I guess — my gut says it's rarer than 1/6 though.
Your gut is right, and we'll compute it properly soon.
Deal.

remaining.description: Multiply probabilities of independent events
Calculate probability of an event NOT happening

completed.description: Form plural nouns in Spanish

context.text: Make gato plural.
gatos — just add s after a vowel.
Right. And flor?
flores, ends in a consonant so it takes es.
Exactly. By the way, adjectives follow along too — watch: el gato negro becomes los gatos negros.
los gatos negros — got it, that's the example you just showed.
Yes — making adjectives agree like that is our next milestone. One more plural first: luz?
luces — the z flips to c.

remaining.description: Make adjectives agree in gender and number with nouns
Use possessive adjectives like mi and tu

completed.description: Print output to the screen with print()

context.text: Make Python say: I am learning.
print("I am learning")
Exactly. What do the quotes do?
They mark the text as a string, so Python doesn't try to run the words as code.
Right. And print(7 + 2) shows?
9 — no quotes, so it does the math first.

remaining.description: Read user input with input()
Join strings together with +

completed.description: Write a function with parameters and call it

context.text: Write a function greet that takes a name and logs Hi <name>.
function greet(name) { console.log('Hi ' + name); }
Good. Now call it for Priya.
greet('Priya')
What prints?
Hi Priya.
Exactly — the argument flows into the parameter.
Makes sense, the parameter is just a placeholder until you call it.

remaining.description: Delay code with setTimeout
Handle asynchronous results with async/await
Attach functions as event handlers

completed.description: Explain what a primary key is and why tables need one

context.text: The students table has id, name, email. Which should be the primary key and why?
id — it uniquely identifies each row, and two students could share a name.
Good. Could email work instead?
Technically it's unique, but people change emails, and a key shouldn't change.
Well reasoned. Can a primary key be NULL?
No, every row has to have one — otherwise you couldn't point at that row.

remaining.description: Create a new table with CREATE TABLE
Link tables with foreign keys

completed.description: Embed an image on a page with the img tag

context.text: Show a photo saved at cat.png with a description for screen readers.
Perfect. Why does img not need a closing tag?
It's a void element — it has no content inside, everything lives in the attributes.
Exactly. What happens if the file path is wrong?
A broken image icon shows, and the alt text appears instead.

remaining.description: Build data tables with table, tr, and td
Create a form with input fields and a submit button
Group content with div and span

completed.description: Format cells as currency

context.text: Column D shows 1499.5 but you want it as money. What do you do?
Select the column, then Format Cells, choose Currency — it becomes $1,499.50.
Right. Does that change the underlying number?
No, it only changes how it's displayed; formulas still see 1499.5.
Exactly the key insight. And the decimal places?
You can set them in the same dialog, usually two for money.

remaining.description: Create a bar chart from a data range
Highlight cells automatically with conditional formatting

completed.description: Read frequencies from a histogram

context.text: This histogram of commute times: the 20-30 minute bar reaches 12. What does that mean?
12 people have commutes between 20 and 30 minutes.
Right. Which interval is most common if the 10-20 bar hits 15?
10 to 20 minutes — tallest bar wins.
And roughly how many people total, if the bars read 5, 15, 12, 8?
40, just add the bar heights.
Exactly.
Histograms are friendlier than I expected.

remaining.description: Construct a box plot from five-number summary
Calculate the interquartile range

completed.description: Evaluate expressions using the order of operations

context.text: What is 2 + 3 * 4?
14 — multiplication first, so 12 plus 2.
Good. Now (2 + 3) * 4?
20, the parentheses force the addition first.
15. Division beats subtraction, so 18 minus 3.
Perfect, three for three.
PEMDAS finally sticks.

remaining.description: Factor quadratic expressions
Solve a system of two linear equations

completed.description: Count beats in 4/4 time

context.text: In 4/4, how many beats fill one measure?
Four, and the quarter note gets one beat.
Right. So a half note plus two quarter notes — does that fill a measure?
What about three half notes?
Too much — that's 6 beats, it overflows into the next measure.
Exactly.
It's just adding fractions of the measure, really.

remaining.description: Identify key signatures from sharps and flats
Build a natural minor scale

completed.description: Count from 1 to 20 in Spanish

context.text: Count from one to five for me.
uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco
Perfect. Now the tricky teens — 11 to 15?
once, doce, trece, catorce, quince
Nailed them. And 16?
dieciseis — literally ten-and-six.
Exactly, and that pattern carries you to 19.
So diecisiete, dieciocho, diecinueve, then veinte is 20.

remaining.description: Tell time in Spanish with es la / son las
Say the days of the week

completed.description: Convert between grams and moles using molar mass

context.text: Water's molar mass is 18 g/mol. How many moles in 36 grams?
Correct. And going the other way — grams in 0.5 mol of water?
0.5 times 18 is 9 grams.
Right. What does molar mass even represent?
The mass of one mole — 6.022 x 10^23 particles — of the substance.
Exactly.
Divide to get moles, multiply to get grams. Easy to remember.

remaining.description: Calculate the molarity of a solution
Balance redox equations

completed.description: Tell potential energy apart from kinetic energy

context.text: A book on a high shelf versus a rolling bowling ball — which energy does each have?
The book has potential energy from its height, the ball has kinetic energy from its motion.
Right. What about a roller coaster car paused at the top of a hill?
Mostly potential — it converts to kinetic on the way down.
And at the bottom, moving fastest?
Almost all kinetic.
Exactly.
Height means stored, motion means active. Got it.

remaining.description: Calculate kinetic energy with KE = 1/2 mv^2
Apply conservation of energy to solve problems

completed.description: Recognize passive voice sentences

context.text: 'The ball was thrown by Sam.' Active or passive?
Passive — the ball receives the action but sits in the subject spot.
Right. 'Sam threw the ball'?
Active, the doer leads.
'Mistakes were made'?
Passive, and sneaky — it hides who made them.
Excellent catch, that's exactly why writers watch for it.
Passive spotting is basically asking: who's doing this, and are they missing?

remaining.description: Rewrite passive sentences in active voice
Vary sentence length to improve rhythm

completed.description: Slice a list with start and end indexes

context.text: Given letters = ['a','b','c','d','e'], what does letters[1:4] give?
['b', 'c', 'd'] — start is included, end is cut off.
Exactly. How would you grab just the first two?
letters[0:2], or letters[:2] for short.
And letters[2:]?
['c', 'd', 'e'], from index 2 to the end.
Perfect.
The half-open interval thing finally makes sense.

remaining.description: Access items from the end with negative indexes
Sort a list with sorted()

completed.description: Build strings with template literals

context.text: You have const city = 'Oslo'. Produce 'Welcome to Oslo!' with a template literal.
`Welcome to ${city}!`
Right. What kind of quotes are those?
Backticks — regular quotes won't do the ${} substitution.
Can you put an expression inside, not just a variable?
Yes, like `Total: ${3 * 7}` gives Total: 21.
Exactly.
So much nicer than gluing strings with plus signs.

remaining.description: Destructure properties out of an object
Copy and merge arrays with the spread operator

Bravo! You can now add and subtract fractions confidently, even the tricky ones with different denominators.
Look at that — you made it all the way through! You can now conjugate Spanish verbs in the present tense and build real sentences about what people do.
Well done, truly. You can now use VLOOKUP to pull matching values out of big tables in seconds — a skill that saves hours of scrolling.
Amazing — you finished strong! You can now transform, filter, and reduce arrays in JavaScript like it's second nature.
You made it! You can now explain the full water cycle — evaporation, condensation, precipitation — and how water keeps moving around our planet.
Huge congrats — you saw this one through to the end. You can now read note values and count out rhythms straight from the page.
What a finish! You can now balance chemical equations, keeping every atom accounted for on both sides of the arrow.
Nicely done — that was no small climb. You can now explain and apply all three of Newton's laws to real moving objects.
Take a bow — you've earned it. You can now write a sharp, arguable thesis statement that gives any essay a real backbone.
Incredible work! You can now build HTML forms from scratch, complete with inputs, labels, and a working submit button.
You crushed it! You can now calculate standard deviation and actually explain what it says about how spread out data is.
Way to go — that's the whole lesson conquered. You can now create, switch, and merge Git branches to keep your work organized and safe.
Beautifully done. You can now explain photosynthesis from sunlight to sugar, and why the whole living world depends on it.
That was impressive to watch. You can now solve linear equations step by step, isolating the unknown with total confidence.
Cheers to you — every milestone, done! You can now work out probabilities for dice and coins, from single rolls to combined events.
Outstanding! You can now place commas exactly where they belong, turning muddled sentences into clear ones.
You nailed it, start to finish. You can now build a working budget in a spreadsheet, tracking income and spending with live formulas.
